questions.question_text: What form is required for a valid sale of an immovable in Louisiana?
questions.correct_answer: Authentic act or act under private signature
distractors: An unsigned memorandum recorded in the conveyance records
An oral agreement before two witnesses
A handshake agreement confirmed by the listing broker
questions.explanation: Civil Code article 2440 requires that a sale of an immovable be made by authentic act or by act under private signature. (Exceptionally, under article 1839 an oral transfer is valid between the parties only if the property has actually been delivered and the transferor admits the transfer under oath.) Recording in the parish conveyance records is not required for validity between the parties, but the sale is without effect against third persons until recorded (articles 1839, 2442, 3338).

questions.question_text: In Louisiana, when does ownership of an immovable transfer between buyer and seller?
questions.correct_answer: When the parties agree on the thing and price
distractors: When the deed is physically delivered
When the act is recorded in the parish
When the buyer takes physical possession
questions.explanation: Under Louisiana civil law, ownership of an immovable transfers between the parties upon agreement as to the thing and the price. This differs from common law, where delivery of the deed typically transfers title. Recording is required only to affect third parties.

questions.question_text: A seller sells an immovable knowing it has hidden foundation damage but does not disclose. Six months later, the buyer discovers the defect. What remedy is available?
questions.correct_answer: Redhibition action against the seller
distractors: No remedy because the sale is final
Only a claim for price reduction from the parish
A negligence suit against the home inspector
questions.explanation: Louisiana's warranty against redhibitory defects protects buyers against hidden defects that existed at the time of sale. Because this seller knew of the defect (bad faith), the buyer's redhibition action prescribes one year from the day the defect was discovered, but no later than ten years from the sale (Civil Code article 2534(B), as revised by Act 414 of 2021). A seller who knows of a defect and fails to disclose it cannot rely on a waiver or 'as is' clause to escape liability (article 2548).

questions.question_text: Under Civil Code article 2534, when does a redhibition action against a seller who did NOT know of the defect prescribe?
questions.correct_answer: 2 years from delivery or 1 year from discovery, whichever occurs first
distractors: 4 years from delivery or 1 year from discovery, whichever occurs first
1 year from the date of the act of sale in all cases
10 years from the perfection of the contract of sale
questions.explanation: Act 414 of 2021 rewrote Civil Code article 2534 to create uniform prescriptive periods for movables and immovables. Against a good-faith seller, a redhibition action prescribes in two years from the day of delivery or one year from the day the buyer discovered the defect, whichever occurs first (article 2534(A)). Against a seller who knew or is presumed to have known of the defect, it prescribes in one year from discovery, but never more than ten years from perfection of the sale (article 2534(B)). The former 4-year rule and the special one-year-from-delivery rule for residential immovables were repealed.

questions.question_text: A seller sells an immovable for $80,000 when its fair market value is $200,000. The buyer paid in good faith. What doctrine might the seller invoke?
questions.correct_answer: Lesion beyond moiety
distractors: Redhibition for hidden defects
Revocatory action for fraud
Nullity for lack of consent
questions.explanation: Lesion beyond moiety allows a seller of an immovable to rescind the sale if the price is less than one-half of the fair market value at the time of sale. Here, $80,000 is less than half of $200,000, so the seller may seek rescission.

questions.question_text: In a bond for deed contract, who retains title to the immovable?
questions.correct_answer: The seller until full payment
distractors: The buyer from the date of the contract
A neutral escrow agent
The parish clerk of court
questions.explanation: A bond for deed is an installment sale contract where the seller retains title to the immovable until the buyer completes all required payments. The buyer receives possession and the right to use the property during the contract term.

questions.question_text: A buyer under a bond for deed contract misses two payments. What right does Louisiana law provide the buyer?
questions.correct_answer: A right to cure the default
distractors: Automatic forfeiture of all payments made
Immediate transfer of title to the seller
A right to renegotiate the purchase price
questions.explanation: Louisiana law protects bond for deed buyers by providing a right to cure a default before the seller can cancel the contract. The seller must give proper notice, and the buyer has an opportunity to bring payments current.

questions.question_text: What happens to a mineral servitude in Louisiana after 10 years of nonuse?
questions.correct_answer: It prescribes and reverts to the landowner
distractors: It automatically renews for 10 more years
It converts to a mineral royalty
It remains valid indefinitely
questions.explanation: A mineral servitude in Louisiana prescribes (expires) after 10 years of nonuse. If no drilling, mining, or production occurs within that period, the mineral rights revert to the landowner. This is a distinctive feature of Louisiana mineral law.

questions.question_text: An unrecorded bond for deed contract exists between a seller and buyer. The seller then sells the immovable to a third party who records. Who prevails?
questions.correct_answer: The third party who recorded the sale
distractors: The original buyer under the bond for deed
Neither party until a court decides
The seller retains ownership
questions.explanation: Louisiana's public records doctrine (Civil Code article 3338) makes an unrecorded instrument affecting an immovable without effect as to third persons. Because the bond for deed was not recorded, the third-party purchaser who recorded prevails — and under McDuffie v. Walker (1909), it does not matter whether the third party knew about the unrecorded contract; knowledge is not the equivalent of registry. Recording the bond for deed (R.S. 9:2941.1) would have protected the buyer.

questions.question_text: Can mineral rights be separated from surface ownership in Louisiana?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, mineral rights are severable
distractors: No, minerals always belong to the surface owner
Only with parish approval
Only for state-owned immovables
questions.explanation: Louisiana law permits mineral rights to be severed from surface ownership through mineral servitudes, mineral royalties, or mineral leases. This allows separate parties to hold surface and mineral interests in the same immovable.

questions.question_text: Which privilege has the highest priority under Louisiana law?
questions.correct_answer: Tax privileges
distractors: Vendor's privilege
Builder's privilege
Conventional mortgage
questions.explanation: Tax privileges (liens) take first priority over all other privileges and mortgages on immovable property in Louisiana. This ensures that parish and state tax claims are satisfied before other creditors.

questions.question_text: What distinguishes executory foreclosure from ordinary foreclosure in Louisiana?
questions.correct_answer: Executory is faster and requires an authentic act
distractors: Executory requires a full trial on the merits
Ordinary is used only for commercial property
Executory allows a statutory redemption period
questions.explanation: Executory process is a streamlined foreclosure that does not require a full trial. It is available only when the mortgage is by authentic act containing a confession of judgment. Ordinary process requires a full judicial proceeding and is slower.

questions.question_text: After a judicial sale of an immovable through foreclosure in Louisiana, does the borrower have a right of redemption?
questions.correct_answer: No, there is no statutory redemption
distractors: Yes, within 6 months of the sale
Yes, within 1 year of the sale
Yes, but only for homestead properties
questions.explanation: Louisiana does not provide a statutory right of redemption after a judicial sale through foreclosure. Once the property is sold at a sheriff's sale, the borrower loses all rights to redeem the property.

questions.question_text: Under Louisiana law (R.S. 9:3500), what is the maximum rate of conventional (contract) interest that parties may generally agree to?
questions.correct_answer: 12% per annum
distractors: 8% per annum
10% per annum
15% per annum
questions.explanation: R.S. 9:3500(C)(1) caps conventional interest at 12 percent per annum, and the rate must be fixed in writing. The cap is riddled with exceptions: it does not apply to consumer credit transactions as defined by the Louisiana Consumer Credit Law, which permits higher, tiered finance charges (R.S. 9:3500(C)(3)(b), (C)(4)(b)), to loans for commercial or business purposes (R.S. 9:3500(D)), or to many licensed and federally regulated lenders.

questions.question_text: A contractor completes major renovations on an immovable but is not paid. The contractor files a privilege. A bank holds a prior-recorded mortgage. Who has priority for the value of the improvements?
questions.correct_answer: The bank's prior-recorded mortgage on the entire property
distractors: The contractor's privilege, but only up to the value added by the improvements
They share pro rata in all sale proceeds
The parish tax collector takes all proceeds
questions.explanation: Under the Private Works Act (R.S. 9:4821(A)(2)), a contractor's privilege is inferior to bona fide mortgages that were effective as to third persons before the privilege arose. Because the bank's mortgage was recorded before the work began, it primes the contractor's privilege in full — current law contains no 'enhanced value' carve-out for contractors. Only privileges for ad valorem taxes and the wage privileges of laborers and employees (R.S. 9:4801(2), 9:4802(A)(2)) outrank such a prior mortgage.

questions.question_text: What is the homestead exemption amount for Louisiana property taxes?
questions.correct_answer: $75,000 of fair market value
distractors: $50,000 of fair market value
$100,000 of fair market value
$25,000 of fair market value
questions.explanation: Louisiana provides a homestead exemption on the first $75,000 of fair market value of a primary residence. At the 10% residential assessment ratio, this equals $7,500 of assessed value exempt from parish ad valorem taxes.

questions.question_text: At what percentage of fair market value are residential immovables assessed in Louisiana?
questions.correct_answer: 10% of fair market value
distractors: 15% of fair market value
25% of fair market value
100% of fair market value
questions.explanation: The Louisiana Constitution (article VII, section 18(B)) sets assessment ratios: land and residential improvements are assessed at 10 percent of fair market value, 'other property' such as commercial buildings at 15 percent, and public service property (excluding land) at 25 percent. So an owner-occupied home is assessed at 10 percent, while a commercial building is assessed at 15 percent (its land still at 10 percent).

questions.question_text: Since January 1, 2026, delinquent Louisiana property taxes result in a tax lien certificate auctioned to investors. How long after the certificate is recorded does the owner have to redeem before the lienholder may sue to enforce the lien?
questions.correct_answer: 3 years from the date of recording
distractors: 1 year from the date of the auction
5 years from the date of the auction
6 months from the delinquency notice
questions.explanation: Act 774 of 2024 and Act 411 of 2025, with constitutional Amendment 4 (approved December 2024), replaced Louisiana's tax sales with tax lien certificate auctions effective January 1, 2026 — bidders now bid down the monthly interest rate (1 percent per month maximum) rather than acquiring an interest in the property. The certificate holder may sue to enforce the lien only after the later of 3 years from recordation of the certificate or 6 months after the notices required by R.S. 47:2156, and the action must be brought within 7 years of recordation (R.S. 47:2155(C)). Even after suit is filed, the owner may still extinguish the lien by paying the termination price until 30 days after being served (R.S. 47:2266.1(D)(1)), and by contradictory motion up until the judicial sale. Tax sales conducted before 2026 remain subject to the former 3-year redemptive period running from recordation of the tax sale certificate.

questions.question_text: A residential lease with a one-year term expires, neither party gives notice, and the tenant remains in possession. What occurs under Louisiana law?
questions.correct_answer: The lease renews month-to-month by reconduction
distractors: The tenant must vacate immediately
The lease renews for one full year
The landlord may change terms without notice
questions.explanation: Under Civil Code article 2721, a fixed-term lease is reconducted when, after the term expires, the lessee remains in possession for one week without notice to vacate or other opposition from either party. Under article 2723, the reconducted lease of a residential term of a month or longer continues from month to month on the same terms and conditions — tacit reconduction is a continuation of the existing lease, not a new one-year renewal.

questions.question_text: Within what time period must a Louisiana landlord return a tenant's security deposit after lease termination?
questions.correct_answer: Within 1 month
distractors: Within 14 days
Within 45 days
Within 60 days
questions.explanation: Louisiana law requires landlords to return the tenant's security deposit within 1 month after the lease terminates and the tenant vacates. The landlord may deduct for damages beyond normal wear and tear but must provide an itemized statement.
